title: Quantum Superposition

theory.introduction: Superposition is one of the most important ideas in quantum computing. It means a qubit can exist in a combination of multiple states at the same time, rather than being fixed as only 0 or 1.
theory.start_from_zero: In classical computing, a bit is always either 0 or 1. There is no in-between. But a qubit can be in a mixture of both states until we measure it.
theory.why_it_matters: Superposition allows quantum computers to process many possibilities at once. This is the foundation of quantum speedup and enables powerful algorithms.
theory.core_idea: A qubit in superposition is described as a weighted combination of |0⟩ and |1⟩, where the weights are called probability amplitudes.
key_points: A qubit can exist in a combination of |0⟩ and |1⟩
Probabilities are obtained by squaring amplitudes
Superposition is not the same as classical randomness
step_by_step_understanding: Step 1: Start with a qubit in |0⟩
Step 4: It now has probabilities for 0 and 1
Step 5: Measurement collapses it to one outcome

math.explanation: α and β are amplitudes. Their squared values give probabilities.

intuition.coin: Like a spinning coin — not heads or tails until observed.
intuition.wave: A qubit behaves like a wave spread across possibilities.
intuition.not_classical_random: Unlike a random bit, a qubit truly exists in a combined state until measured.

sections.title: Equal Superposition
content: The most common superposition is created using the Hadamard gate

sections.title: Unequal Superposition
content: Superposition does not have to be equal
The qubit is more likely to be measured as |0⟩

sections.title: Superposition vs Classical Randomness
content: A classical random bit is either 0 or 1 (unknown to us)
A qubit in superposition is truly in a combination of states
Quantum systems can show interference effects, unlike classical randomness

sections.title: Bloch Sphere Interpretation
content: Superposition corresponds to points on the surface of the Bloch sphere
Equal superposition lies on the equator
Different superpositions correspond to different positions on the sphere

sections.title: Superposition and Parallelism
content: A qubit in superposition represents multiple possibilities at once
Quantum algorithms use this to explore many paths simultaneously
However, measurement gives only one result, so algorithms must use interference cleverly

common_mistakes: Thinking a qubit is literally both 0 and 1 physically
Ignoring amplitudes and focusing only on probabilities
Assuming superposition means we get both outputs after measurement

try_this: Measure multiple times and observe distribution
Apply H twice and observe return to |0⟩
Apply Z after H and observe phase change

quiz.question: What is superposition?
options: A qubit being in a combination of states
quiz.answer: A qubit being in a combination of states

quiz.question: Is superposition the same as classical randomness?
quiz.explanation: Superposition is a quantum phenomenon with interference effects.
